current_fact.fact_text: On May 2, 2012, while "The Scream" was making headlines, another story of sound and emotion had long been evolving: by that same year, streaming and digital playback were transforming how we listen, making iconic images and songs endlessly replayable and remixable. Munch’s cry and modern playlists both keep cycling through our collective nervous system.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

current_fact.match_reason: An artwork becoming a hyper-commodified, endlessly reproduced symbol parallels an episode that questions how we consume infinite, repeatable music and images.

queue.fact_text: On May 2, 2008, the Chaitén eruption also offered volcanologists a rare chance to watch a rhyolitic volcano awaken in a modern, densely instrumented era. Scientists captured high-resolution data on ash plumes, seismic swarms, and lava dome growth, turning a dangerous event into a natural laboratory.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: A volcano becoming a vast open-air classroom for scientists matches an episode celebrating curiosity, data, and the joy of learning from complex, messy processes.

queue.fact_text: On May 2, 1969, as the Queen Elizabeth 2 headed for New York, the era of glamorous liners was already giving way to mass air travel, turning ocean voyages from necessity into experience. Today, such crossings are less about arriving fast and more about savoring the long, slow rhythm of the sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: A leisurely ocean crossing that survives mainly as a slow travel experience fits an episode about decelerating, breathing, and embracing a slower planet.

queue.fact_text: On May 2, 2000, when high-accuracy GPS became available to civilians, it didn’t just improve navigation—it enabled new forms of art and play, from geo-tagged photography to location-based games. Invisible satellites began quietly drawing lines under our movements, turning the whole planet into a canvas of tracked footsteps.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: A technology that turned Earth into a playground for invisible paths parallels a format that invites listeners to drift without fixed routes, letting sound map the journey.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1963, German engineer Berthold Seliger launched a three-stage sounding rocket near Cuxhaven that climbed beyond 100 kilometers, crossing into space. Remarkably, it remains the only sounding rocket fully developed in Germany, a brief but striking chapter in civilian rocketry experimentation during the Cold War era.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A rocket piercing the edge of space pairs with an episode about reimagining empty space as creative potential and shifting perspectives.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 2000, President Bill Clinton announced the end of "selective availability" on GPS signals, allowing civilian users worldwide to access far more accurate satellite positioning. That quiet policy switch turned a military navigation system into the backbone of everyday technologies, from smartphone maps to precision agriculture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A decision that ultimately put GPS in every smartphone fits an episode about our deep, complicated dependence on digital devices.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1964, climbers made the first ascent of Shishapangma, the fourteenth-highest mountain on Earth and the lowest of the famed 8,000-meter peaks. Even as the “smallest” of the Eight-thousanders, its summit lies in air so thin that human bodies slowly deteriorate just by standing there.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

published.match_reason: A Himalayan first ascent mirrors an episode steeped in mountain landscapes and the emotional altitude of high places.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1969, the famed British ocean liner Queen Elizabeth 2 departed on her maiden voyage from Southampton to New York. Designed in the jet age yet steeped in ocean-liner glamour, she became a floating symbol of transatlantic travel’s transition from necessity to nostalgia.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A legendary liner’s first transatlantic crossing resonates with an episode recorded at sea, celebrating shared journeys over water.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1952, a de Havilland Comet made aviation history by completing the first jetliner flight with fare-paying passengers, from London to Johannesburg. Passengers suddenly experienced a world where continents felt hours closer, ushering in the era of routine high-speed air travel.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

published.match_reason: The birth of commercial jet travel connects with an 'on the move' soundtrack built for journeys and forward motion.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1970, ALM Flight 980 ditched in the Caribbean Sea near Saint Croix after multiple failed landing attempts, in one of the earliest airline accidents where most passengers survived a controlled water landing. The incident became a crucial case study in ditching procedures and life-raft survival.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

published.match_reason: A dramatic landing on the sea mirrors an episode themed around water, immersion, and the tension between danger and calm below the surface.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1808, the people of Madrid rose in a spontaneous uprising against Napoleon’s occupying forces, an event later immortalized by Francisco Goya in "The Second of May" and "The Third of May 1808." The rebellion failed militarily but ignited the Peninsular War and etched an image of civilian resistance into art history.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

published.match_reason: A Madrid uprising that shaped Spain’s identity fits with an episode tracing another Spanish cultural force: the history and emotion of flamenco.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1867, zoologist Albert Günther published a study recognizing that New Zealand’s tuatara is not a lizard but the last survivor of an entirely separate reptile lineage, the Rhynchocephalia. This unassuming, nocturnal creature turned out to be a living fossil, preserving a branch of reptile evolution otherwise lost.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A scientific revelation about a rare, ancient animal complements an episode centered on unique, charismatic wildlife and the wonder of nonhuman lives.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1986, the city of Chernobyl was evacuated six days after the nuclear disaster at the nearby power plant, transforming a lived-in Soviet town into a frozen-in-time exclusion zone. Nature has since crept back through abandoned streets, creating a haunting experiment in post-human ecosystems.

episode.title: Morning Drops #19 - Wabi Sabi
episode.description: Wabi-sabi is a story about a handmade mug, a small mistake, and the idea that imperfection can be something to keep—not correct. In this Morning Drop, Gaia shares a moment that didn’t go to plan, and four tracks that echo the same spirit: rough takes, studio accidents, lo-fi textures, and quiet surprises. A soft, thoughtful selection moving between warm electronics, classic riffs, and raw edges.A reminder that not everything needs to be polished. Sometimes, it’s better that way.

published.match_reason: A catastrophic failure that left behind an eerie, reclaimed landscape pairs with an episode meditating on imperfection, unintended consequences, and strange beauty in what went wrong.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 2008, Chile’s Chaitén volcano erupted explosively after more than 9,000 years of dormancy, forcing the evacuation of the nearby town. Its ash plume climbed over 20 kilometers into the sky, reminding modern Chileans that even "quiet" volcanoes can wake abruptly.

episode.title: Morning Drops #46 - Fog and Fire
episode.description: In today’s Morning Drops, Gaia brings us on a passport-free trip between two Italian energies: the foggy stillness of the North and the volcanic pulse of the South. Throughout the journey, Gaia shares four of her favourite Italian songwriters. Grab your coffee and tune into the radio show from another planet!
songs: Edoardo Bennato - La Torre Di Babele
Lucio Dalla - Com'è Profondo il Mare

published.match_reason: An ancient volcano’s sudden awakening aligns with an episode about the fiery, volcanic energy of the south and the unseen forces simmering under everyday life.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 2008, Cyclone Nargis made landfall in Myanmar, driving a storm surge up the Irrawaddy Delta that killed over 138,000 people and left millions homeless. It was one of the deadliest tropical cyclones ever recorded in the North Indian Ocean, exposing how fragile low-lying river deltas are to rising seas and extreme weather.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A devastating storm with relentless rain and wind is contrasted with an episode about weathering storms, literal and metaphorical, and trusting that 'it can't rain forever.'

published.fact_text: On May 2, 2012, a pastel version of Edvard Munch’s "The Scream" sold at auction in New York for about $120 million, briefly setting a world record for a work of art sold at auction. The image of a figure clutching its face against a swirling sky thus became not just a symbol of modern anxiety, but of the extremes of the art market.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: An iconic depiction of raw emotion and inner turmoil connects with an episode celebrating powerful voices that channel complex feelings into art.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1945, the U.S. 82nd Airborne Division liberated the Wöbbelin concentration camp in Germany, finding around 1,000 dead prisoners and thousands more starving survivors. Photographs and testimonies from Wöbbelin played a key role in forcing the world to confront the realities of Nazi camps.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: The liberation of a concentration camp and the need to remember its horrors fits an episode about music as political protest and the refusal to look away.

published.fact_text: Also on May 2, 1945, the segregated all-Nisei 522nd Field Artillery Battalion of the U.S. Army halted a death march from Dachau in southern Bavaria, saving hundreds of prisoners. These Japanese American soldiers were liberating Nazi prisoners even as many of their own families remained incarcerated in U.S. internment camps.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: Japanese American soldiers liberating prisoners while their own rights were denied echoes Residente’s themes of resistance, identity, and contradictions inside systems of power.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1941, Britain launched the Anglo-Iraqi War after a coup in Baghdad deposed the pro-British regent, Crown Prince 'Abd al-Ilah. In a brief but consequential campaign, British and Commonwealth forces reasserted control, entangling Iraq’s future with imperial geopolitics and oil.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A war fought in Iraq’s deserts and cities aligns with an episode that sonically wanders through medinas and Arabic-influenced soundscapes.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1989, Hungary quietly began dismantling its border fence with Austria, opening a physical gap in the Iron Curtain. That decision allowed East Germans to slip westward and helped set off a chain of events that culminated in the fall of the Berlin Wall later that year.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: Taking down a border fence and letting people cross freely fits with an episode about breaking boxes, categories, and constraints.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1995, during the Croatian War of Independence, forces of the Republic of Serb Krajina fired cluster munitions into Zagreb, killing seven civilians and injuring more than 175. The attack later became part of international war crimes prosecutions, underscoring the enduring controversy around cluster weapons.

episode.title: Morning Drops #51 - The Dancefloor
episode.description:
For International Women’s Day, this Morning Drop turns its focus to women on the dancefloor, not as a trend, but as a cultural force.

Across five tracks, Gaia follows a line from disco closers to hip hop club classics, tracing how the dancefloor became a place where women could take space, build confidence, and create connection, sometimes with friends, sometimes alone, always with music at the centre.

This episode isn’t about pointing fingers. It’s a gentle reminder that spaces like the dancefloor still matter: places where people can express themselves, meet each other with respect, and feel part of something bigger than a night out.

Tracklist:
Donna Summer - Last Dance (1978)
The Sequence  - Funk you up
Salt-N-Pepa - Push It
Queen Latifah - Ladies First
Alicia Bridges - I Love the Nightlife

published.match_reason: A city struck by indiscriminate weapons contrasts starkly with the idea of a safe, respectful dancefloor, subtly highlighting how fragile peaceful public spaces can be.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1982, during the Falklands War, the British nuclear submarine HMS Conqueror torpedoed and sank the Argentine cruiser ARA General Belgrano outside a declared exclusion zone. The controversial attack killed over 300 sailors and remains heavily debated as a turning point that shifted the momentum of the conflict at sea.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, unseen submarine strike that sent shockwaves through a war connects metaphorically to an episode themed around electric jolts and high-voltage energy.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 2011, U.S. Navy SEALs killed al-Qaeda leader Osama bin Laden in a compound in Abbottabad, Pakistan, after a decade-long manhunt following the September 11 attacks. The covert raid instantly became one of the most scrutinized special operations in modern history and reshaped narratives around terrorism and justice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #53 - Tyler, The Creator
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin follows the evolution of Tyler, the Creator, from the kid nobody took seriously to one of the most important artists of his generation. From a cockroach eaten on camera as a joke to Grammy stages shared with his mother, this episode traces a career built on stubbornness, reinvention, and the quiet courage of refusing to stay in one place.

Listen, move, and let no one put you in a box.

Tracklist:
Tyler, the Creator – Like Him (feat. Lola Young)
Tyler, the Creator – Yonkers
Tyler, the Creator – See You Again (feat. Kali Uchis)
Tyler, the Creator – Earfquake
Tyler, the Creator – Ring Ring Ring

published.match_reason: A figure who defined a generation of geopolitical fear pairs with an episode about an artist defined by constant reinvention, both reshaping global narratives in very different ways.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 1625, Portuguese Jesuit Afonso Mendes arrived at Beilul from Goa to assume his role as Latin Patriarch of Ethiopia, appointed by Pope Gregory XV. His mission to align the Ethiopian Church with Roman Catholic doctrine ignited fierce religious and political tensions that reshaped Ethiopia’s relationship with Europe.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A story of European religious influence in Africa resonates with an episode centered on African cultural power radiating outward through music.

published.fact_text: On May 2, 2012, while "The Scream" was making headlines, another story of sound and emotion had long been evolving: by that same year, streaming and digital playback were transforming how we listen, making iconic images and songs endlessly replayable and remixable. Munch’s cry and modern playlists both keep cycling through our collective nervous system.

episode.description:

In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.
